1: Psalm 100's superscription mizmôr lĕtôdāh (a psalm for the thank-offering) specifically designates this as the psalm accompanying the tôdāh (thanksgiving/thank-offering) — the particular type of peace offering (šĕlāmîm) presented when a person sought to thank God for specific deliverance (Leviticus 7:12-15; Psalms 22:29). The tôdāh required a public celebration, communal eating of the sacrificial meal in the temple courts, and songs of thanksgiving. Psalm 100 is thus the processional hymn sung as worshippers entered the sanctuary to present the tôdāh feast. "Shout for joy (hārîʿû) to the LORD, all the earth" — the identical opening to Psalms 98:4; the universal scope means all of creation is implicitly included in the thanksgiving processional.
2: "Serve the LORD with joy (bĕśimḥāh)! Come into his presence with songs of praise (birʿānānāh)!" — ʿābad (to serve, to work, to worship) encompasses the full range of covenant service: labor, worship, and obedience are all contained in the single verb. "With joy" — the joy of covenantal service as distinct from slavish obligation; Deuteronomy 28:47-48 identifies joyless service as the spiritual condition that opens the door to covenant curse: "because you did not serve the LORD your God joyfully... you will serve the enemies the LORD sends against you." "Come into his presence (bōʾû lĕpānāyw)" — the approach to the divine face, the sanctuary as the place of divine face-presence (pānîm). "Songs of praise (rĕnānāh)" — the ringing, jubilant cry that accompanies the processional approach.
3: "Know (dĕʿû) that the LORD is God! He made us (hûʾ ʿāśānû), and we belong to him (wĕlô ʾănaḥnû); we are his people, the flock he tends (ṣōʾn marʿîṯô)" — the imperative "know" (yādaʿ) — covenantal knowing, not merely intellectual. Three claims are stacked: YHWH is God (his nature), he made us (his creative act), and we are his (the covenant relationship). "We belong to him (lô ʾănaḥnû)" — there is a textual variant: the written text (kĕtîb) has lōʾ (not: "and not ourselves we") while the traditional reading (qĕrê) has lô (to him). The qĕrê is almost universally followed. The shepherd metaphor ("flock he tends") connects to the entire OT shepherd-king tradition climaxing in Jesus's "I am the good shepherd" (John 10:14).
4: "Enter his gates (šĕʿārāyw) with thanksgiving (bĕtôdāh) and his courts (ḥăṣērōṯāyw) with praise (bĕtĕhillāh)! Thank him and bless his name!" — the processional instructions for the tôdāh offering: the worshipper moves inward through the gates (outer wall) and courts (the open areas between gate and sanctuary), each threshold marked by the intensification of praise. The gate → courts → sanctuary movement mirrors the increasing holiness of concentric spaces in the Israelite sanctuary. "Bless his name (bārĕkû šĕmô)" — divine blessing directed toward the name; the name is the target of blessing as it is the source of blessing (Numbers 6:24-27; Psalms 103:1-2; Psalms 145:1-2). The name contains and represents all that God is and has done.
5: "For the LORD is good (kî-ṭôb YHWH); his steadfast love (ḥasdô) endures forever (lĕʿôlām), and his faithfulness (weʾĕmûnāṯô) reaches to every generation" — the theological foundation of the entire processional: the tôdāh is grounded in divine character, not merely in specific past acts. The three divine attributes — goodness, ḥesed, and faithfulness — span time (forever, every generation) in a way that specific acts do not. Ḥesed enduring "forever (lĕʿôlām)" is the great refrain of Ps 136, where it punctuates 26 specific historical acts. Here it is stated without the specific acts — divine covenant loyalty is the permanent backdrop against which all specific deliverances occur.
